sections.a_description: Pure form-follows-function philosophy born in 1919 Weimar Germany, uniting fine art with industrial craft through geometric shapes, primary colors, and bold typography.
sections.ai_prompt: Design a UI in Bauhaus style. Use strictly geometric shapes (circles, squares, triangles). Color palette: black #0A0A0A, Bauhaus red #C41A07, Bauhaus yellow #F5BF00, Bauhaus blue #103DBF, off-white #FAFAF7, cream #EAE3CE. Typography: Bebas Neue or any bold condensed sans-serif for headlines, Barlow or geometric sans-serif for body text. Apply a strong grid structure. Use flat color blocks without gradients — only hard offset box-shadows (e.g. 8px 8px 0 #0A0A0A) for depth. Labels: uppercase with wide letter-spacing (2px+). Zero decorative ornament — every element must serve a functional purpose. Borders: 2px solid black. Accent stripe: yellow vertical bar on card left edge. Navigation: black bar with white type.

colors.label: Off-White
colors.note: Card body background, main content area

alt_palettes.name: Muted Bauhaus
alt_palettes.note: Warmer, aged-paper feel for editorial and cultural contexts

alt_palettes.name: Dark Bauhaus
alt_palettes.note: High-contrast dark mode for dashboards and creative tools

fonts.specimen: The Bauhaus unified fine art with industrial design. Every element earns its place.

items.title: Form Follows Function
items.a_description: Every element earns its place. Decoration is eliminated; shape, color, and proportion communicate through geometry alone.

items.title: Grid as Structure
items.a_description: Strict geometric grids underpin every composition. Alignment is not just aesthetic — it is architectural.

items.title: Primary Colors Only
items.a_description: Black, white, red, yellow, blue. Secondaries dilute impact. The primary palette is universal, democratic, and unambiguous.

items.title: Typography as Design
items.a_description: Letters are geometric forms first, readable characters second. Condensed sans-serif headlines carry the same visual weight as structural elements.

items.title: Craft Meets Industry
items.a_description: Fine art and industrial production are inseparable. Beautiful things must be reproducible at scale — handcraft is a method, not a virtue.

items.title: Universal Beauty
items.a_description: Good design is for everyone. Bauhaus rejected elitist decoration in favor of clear, accessible visual language for modern life.

sections.a_description: Bauhaus excels where intellectual seriousness, visual authority, and geometric precision communicate trust and expertise.

paragraphs: Founded in 1919 in Weimar, Germany by architect Walter Gropius, the Bauhaus school set out to reconcile fine art with the machine age of industrial production. It rejected the ornate historicism of the 19th century in favor of a radical new teaching method: students learned both craft and theory, working alongside master craftsmen and fine artists in a unified workshop system.

The school moved to Dessau in 1925, where it reached its creative peak under Gropius, then Hannes Meyer, and finally Ludwig Mies van der Rohe. The faculty included Wassily Kandinsky, Paul Klee, László Moholy-Nagy, Herbert Bayer, and Marcel Breuer — a concentration of modernist talent unmatched in design history. Their work in typography, furniture, weaving, photography, and architecture still defines what we recognize as “modern design.”

Forced to close in 1933 under Nazi pressure, the Bauhaus diaspora spread its influence across Europe and America. Émigrés carried the methodology into Harvard, Yale, and the Illinois Institute of Technology, seeding the Swiss International Style, the New York modernist advertising era, and ultimately the entire post-war graphic design profession. The Bauhaus lasted just 14 years; its aesthetic and educational philosophy has lasted a century and shows no sign of fading.

items.title: Constructivism
items.description: Soviet counterpart to Bauhaus — geometric abstraction, red and black, diagonal compositions, and art in service of political purpose.

items.title: Swiss Graphic Style
items.description: Bauhaus principles systematized into a global design language — the grid made mathematical, typography reduced to Helvetica, white space as doctrine.

items.title: Art Deco
items.description: The glamorous contemporary of Bauhaus — also geometric, but where Bauhaus stripped ornament, Art Deco amplified it with gold, luxury, and symmetrical excess.

items.title: Dutch Design
items.description: De Stijl movement that ran parallel to Bauhaus — Mondrian's primary-color grid logic translated directly into architecture, typography, and object design.
